title: Levels of organisation — cells to organisms
examTip: AQA often asks you to put the levels in order or give an example at each level. Know the hierarchy and at least two examples for animals and one for plants.
intro: Your body has about 37 trillion cells in it. If they all just did their own thing independently, you'd be a blob. The reason you're not is that cells are organised — grouped together, working as teams, forming bigger and bigger structures until you get a functioning human being. This lesson is about those layers, and it's one of the shortest topics in the whole course.

keyPoints.heading: The four levels — smallest to largest
keyPoints.content: Think of it like a building. Bricks on their own are useless. Bricks form walls. Walls form rooms. Rooms form a house. In biology it works the same way: cells group together to form tissues, tissues group together to form organs, and organs work together in organ systems. The whole living thing — you — is the organism. That's the full chain: cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism.

keyPoints.heading: Tissues — cells doing the same job
keyPoints.content: A tissue is a group of cells that are similar in structure and do the same job. Your heart, for example, contains muscular tissue (cells that contract to pump blood), glandular tissue (cells that make and secrete substances), and epithelial tissue (cells that line surfaces). Each type of tissue has a specific function — that's what makes it a tissue rather than just a random collection of cells.

keyPoints.heading: Organs — tissues working together
keyPoints.content: An organ is made of several different tissues working together to do a specific job. The heart is an organ. The stomach is an organ. The leaf of a plant is an organ. Each one contains multiple tissue types, all contributing to one overall function. A common mistake is thinking an organ is just one type of cell — it's not. If it only had one type of cell, it'd be a tissue.

keyPoints.heading: Organ systems — and the digestive system as your main example
keyPoints.content: Organ systems are groups of organs that work together to carry out a major function. The digestive system is your go-to example for this topic — it includes the mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, gall bladder and pancreas, all coordinating to digest food. Other examples: the circulatory system (heart, blood vessels, blood) and the respiratory system (lungs, trachea, diaphragm). AQA also expects you to know that plants have organ systems — roots, stems and leaves are all plant organs.

tables.title: Levels of organisation — with examples
rows: A group of similar cells doing the same job
Several tissues working together for a specific function
A group of organs working together

1.question: What is a tissue?
options: A single cell with a specialised function
A group of similar cells working together to do the same job
A group of organs that form a complete system
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — similar cells, same job. That's the definition AQA wants.
1.wrongFeedback: A tissue is a group of similar cells doing the same job — one step up from individual cells, one step below an organ.

3.question: Which of these is an example of an organ system?
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — the digestive system is a collection of organs (stomach, intestines, liver etc.) working together. That's an organ system.
3.wrongFeedback: The stomach is an organ. Muscular tissue is a tissue. A muscle cell is a cell. The digestive system is the organ system — it's made up of multiple organs all working together.

commonMistakes: Saying the heart is a tissue — it's an organ (it contains multiple tissue types). Don't mix up the levels.
Listing only one tissue type when asked about an organ — organs contain several different tissues.
Forgetting plants in this topic — roots, stems and leaves are organs, and plants have organ systems too.
Getting the order wrong under pressure — write it out: cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism.
examTips: If asked to 'put these in order of organisation', go smallest to largest: cell, tissue, organ, organ system, organism.
For a 'give an example' question, always give a specific named example — 'muscular tissue' not just 'tissue'; 'the digestive system' not just 'a system'.
The digestive system question often asks you to name organs within it — be ready to list at least four: stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver.

videoLinks.label: Free Science Lessons — Levels of Organisation

revisionCardBullets: Order of organisation (smallest → largest): cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism
Cell = the basic unit of life — e.g. muscle cell, palisade mesophyll cell
Tissue = group of similar cells doing the same job — e.g. muscular tissue, epithelial tissue
Organ = several tissues working together for a specific function — e.g. heart, stomach, leaf
Organ system = group of organs working together — e.g. digestive system, circulatory system
The heart contains muscular tissue, glandular tissue and epithelial tissue — it's an organ, not a tissue
Digestive system organs include: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, gall bladder, pancreas
Plants also have organs: leaf, stem and root — and organ systems (e.g. shoot system)
Palisade mesophyll cells → palisade mesophyll tissue → leaf (organ) — the plant version of the hierarchy
